coredata.dc:title: Controllable fabrication of novel graphene quantum dots/fluorinated boron nitride ultralight composites for broadband and high-performance microwave absorption
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Qi
Fang, Yi
Guo, Zhonglu
Du, Zhao
Liu, Zhenya
Yu, Yadi
Tian, Xiaoyu
Tang, Chengchun
coredata.dc:description: The microwave absorption performance of graphene materials is severely hindered by their high dielectric constant and ultralow magnetic loss ability. Herein, for the first time, we report a simple strategy to achieve a suitable electromagnetic match by fabricating fluorinated boron nitride nanosheets supported graphene quantum dots (GQDs/F-BNNs) composites. Our results show that the reduced microwave reflection and enhanced magnetic loss are attributed to the low dielectric constant and ferromagnetism of F-BNNs, which also overcome the typically hydrophilic of GQDs to realize desirable stability in water. Then, we highlight that the absorption bandwidths and reflection loss (RL min) are closely correlated with the size of GQDs, which is introduced by the modulated bandgap widths of GQDs concerning microwave in different frequencies. Thereinto, the optimized GQDs/F-BNNs exhibit excellent microwave absorption performance with the RL min of −66.69 dB with a thin thickness of only 5.29 mm in 2∼18 GHz. Moreover, when the frequency reaches 19∼26 dB and 27∼40 dB, RL values are also both less than −10 dB. Our findings pave the way for GQDs and pioneer F-BNNs as lightweight and high-efficient microwave absorbing materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Fluorinated boron nitride
One-step hydrothermal method
Microwave absorption

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of GQDs with different sizes and GQDs/F-BNNs by hydrothermal method
The possible mechanism of GQDs/F-BNNs for the microwave absorption

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51772075 ), Hebei Provincial Natural Science Foundation ( E2018202129 ) and S&T Program of Hebei ( 199676242H ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthetic scheme for the preparation of (a) F-BNNs, (b) GQDs and (c) GQDs/F-BNNs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a-c) XRD patterns of F-BNNs-1∼F-BNNs-9 under different temperature and time conditions. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
The XPS survey spectrum and B1s, C1s, N1s and F1s fine scans of (a-d) F-BNNs-9. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a-c)TEM, (d) HRTEM of F-BNNs-9. Corresponding elemental mappings of B, C, N and F of F-BNNs-9 taken in the square region in Fig. 4e. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Hysteresis loops measured for F-BNNs-1, F-BNNs-5 and F-BNNs-9 for applied magnetic field between −500 Oe and 500 Oe. Inset: The most stable and optimized structure of F-BNNs. (b) Spin-resolved electronic band structures of F-BNNs-9 calculated using a supercell containing one F ions. (c) Spin-resolved electron density of states projected onto F, B (sp3-hybridized), N (nearest to the sp3-hybridized B) atoms. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a-i) 3D representation of RL curves for F-BNNs-1∼F-BNNs-9 with a certain thickness in the frequency range of 2∼18 GHz. Frequency dependence of (j) ε′, (k) ε″, (l) μ′, (m) μ″, (n) tanδ
E and (o) tanδ
M for F-BNNs-1∼F-BNNs-9. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

TEM images of (a) GO, (b) unshaped GQDs and (c-f) synthesis of GQDs of different sizes. Inset: HRTEM image of GQDs. (g) Photoluminescent (PL) spectrum of GQDs (75 nm, 25 nm, 15 nm and 5 nm) in water (450 nm). (h and i) The GQDs (75 nm, 25 nm, 15 nm and 5 nm) under the natural light and the ultraviolet light (365 nm). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

TEM images of (a) S1, (b) S2, (c) S3, (d and e) S4. (f) HAADF image, energy dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDS) energy spectrum and elemental mapping images of S4. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
3D representation of RL curves for the (a-d) S1, S2, S3 and S4 with a certain thickness in the frequency range of 2∼18 GHz. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

2D images of calculated theoretical RL value of samples for (a) S1, (b) S2, (c) S3 and (d) S4; (e-h) RL of the different samples versus frequency at different thicknesses in the frequency range of 2∼18 GHz. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Frequency dependence of (a) ε′, (b) ε″, (c) μ′, (d) μ″, (e) tanδ
E and (f) tanδ
M for F-BNNs-9, S1, S2, S3 and S4. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
The contrast patterns of (a) α and (b) impedance matching ratio for F-BNNs-9, S1, S2, S3 and S4. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
The RL curves for F-BNNs-9, S1, S2, S3 and S4 in the frequency range of (a) 19∼26 GHz and (b) 27∼40 GHz. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Microwave waves can easily enter F-BNNs and be attenuated in energy. (b) Contact angle test of GQDs/F-BNNs composites. (c) Comparison of microwave wave energy loss of GQDs of different sizes. (d) Microwave absorption model diagram of GQDs/F-BNNs composites. (e) Schematic diagram of microwave absorption mechanism for GQDs/F-BNNs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
The naming rules for F-BNNs samples.
Microwave absorption performance values of this work and related references.

row.entry: Effective band range in 2–18 GHz (RL < −10 dB) (GHz)

head.ce:title: Controllable fabrication of novel graphene quantum dots/fluorinated boron nitride ultralight composites for broadband and high-performance microwave absorption

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Hebei University of Technology, Tianjin, 300130, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Materials Science and Engineering, Hebei University of Technology, Tianjin 300130, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The microwave absorption performance of graphene materials is severely hindered by their high dielectric constant and ultralow magnetic loss ability. Herein, for the first time, we report a simple strategy to achieve a suitable electromagnetic match by fabricating fluorinated boron nitride nanosheets supported graphene quantum dots (GQDs/F-BNNs) composites. Our results show that the reduced microwave reflection and enhanced magnetic loss are attributed to the low dielectric constant and ferromagnetism of F-BNNs, which also overcome the typically hydrophilic of GQDs to realize desirable stability in water. Then, we highlight that the absorption bandwidths and reflection loss (RL min) are closely correlated with the size of GQDs, which is introduced by the modulated bandgap widths of GQDs concerning microwave in different frequencies. Thereinto, the optimized GQDs/F-BNNs exhibit excellent microwave absorption performance with the RL min of −66.69 dB with a thin thickness of only 5.29 mm in 2∼18 GHz. Moreover, when the frequency reaches 19∼26 dB and 27∼40 dB, RL values are also both less than −10 dB. Our findings pave the way for GQDs and pioneer F-BNNs as lightweight and high-efficient microwave absorbing materials.

ce:section.ce:para: Previous researches are mostly dedicated to fabricating two-dimensional (2D) and three-dimensional (3D) graphene and reduced graphene oxide (RGO)-based materials for high absorption radiation interferences [7–12]. For example, Li and co-workers prepared lightweight CeO2−x /RGO composites [13]. Wu et al. designed and synthesized MoO2 nanoparticles that are sandwiched uniformly between porous carbon shells and RGO [14]. Despite significant microwave absorption performance that has been achieved by traditional 2D and 3D microwave absorbers, however, the existential shortcomings, such as high reflection, low absorption, narrow absorption bandwidth, mismatch electromagnetic impedance et al., still severely restrict their further applications. Thus, exploring materials with low density, low dielectric constant and magnetic loss has become a key issue. GQDs have the characteristics of low reflection, continuous absorption band, strong absorption, and adjustable bandgap. The use of GQDs to carry out microwave absorption performance research has become a current hot spot [15]. However, the hydrophilic characteristics of GQDs limit their application in the field of microwave absorption. Therefore, seeking an excellent carrier for GQDs has become an important task. Recently, hexagonal boron nitride (h-BN) materials have the characteristics of low density and low dielectric constant, and it is found as a natural hyperbolic material, and these unique properties enable it as a candidate for practical usages in hyperlenses [16], near-field imaging [17], deep-UV emitters [18] and quantum optoelectronics [19]. Meanwhile, after h-BN is fluorinated, it will have ferromagnetic characteristics. For example, Du et al. proposed the preparation method of F-BNNs and the change characteristics of their magnetic properties and concluded that F-BNNs have obvious ferromagnetic properties [20]. Li et al. used ab initio calculations to know the chemisorption of F atoms on the B atoms of boron nitride nanotubes can induce spontaneous magnetization [21]. Therefore, h-BN is desired to exhibit a promising perspective in the field of microwave absorption.

In this work, we propose to use F-BNNs with low dielectric constants as the substrates and GQDs with strong microwave absorption properties as the load to prepare GQDs/F-BNNs composite materials for microwave absorption. This approach is intended to increase the strong reflection of GQDs and overcome the insufficient microwave absorption capacity of single F-BNNs.

Commercial boron nitride was supplied by Qingzhou Fangyuan Boron Nitride Factory. Graphite powder, hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37%), nitric acid (HNO3, 69%), sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 98%) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30%) were purchased in Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory. Ammonium fluoride (NH4F, 98%), potassium permanganate (KMnO4, 99%), Isopropyl alcohol (C3H8O, 99.7%), Ethylene glycol (C2H6O2, 99%) and Potassium hydroxide (KOH, 85%) was obtained from Tianjin Fengchuan Chemical Reagent Technology Co., Ltd. All chemicals are analytically pure without further purification. Deionized water was used for all experiments.

The preparation process of F-BNNs is shown in Fig. 1 a. The commercial boron nitride and C3H8O (100 mL) were ultrasonically dispersed into a three-necked flask. The mixture in the flask was stirred and heated at 50 °C for 24 h. The precipitate is filtered off, collected in the upper layer solution, centrifuged and dried to obtain white boron nitride nanosheets (BNNs). BNNs (0.5 g) and NH4F (2 g) were ultrasonically dispersed in 40 mL of deionized water and 40 mL C2H6O2 for 1 h. Then, the mixture was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave. The reaction temperature is 180 °C, 210 °C and 240 °C, and the reaction times are 18 h, 24 h and 30 h, respectively. The naming rules of the obtained samples are shown in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of GQDs with different sizes and GQDs/F-BNNs by hydrothermal method
ce:section.ce:para: The graphite powder and KMnO4 are uniformly mixed in a three-necked flask, and H2SO4 and HNO3 are added. The mixture was heated and stirred for 24 h and then cooled in an ice bath. After cooling, H2O2 was added to obtain a yellow suspension, which was washed with HCl and deionized water several times and then freeze-dried to obtain graphene oxide (GO) [22].

The “top-down approaches” is a common method for preparing GQDs. In this work, KOH is used as “scissors” to cut large pieces of GO into small pieces of GQDs, and adjust the time variable to obtain different sizes of GQDs. The preparation method of GQDs of different sizes is shown in Fig. 1b, GO and KOH was ultrasonically dispersed in 80 mL of deionized water for 30 min. Then, the mixture was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave. The reaction temperature is 200 °C, and the reaction times are 5 h, 10 h, 15 h, 20 h and 30 h, respectively. The product was separated by filtration through a 0.22 μm filter to give a light brown solution. The solution was dialyzed to pH = 7 and collected to obtain GQDs (none, 75 nm, 25 nm, 15 nm and 5 nm) [23].

The prepared GQDs were mixed with F-BNNs-9 into the Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and ultrasonically dispersed in 80 mL of deionized water for 1 h (Fig. 1c). Then, stop the ultrasonic and transfer the Teflon-lined stainless autoclave to the oven, and set the temperature and time to 200 °C for 10 h. After cool about to room temperature naturally, a brownish-white powder was obtained by centrifugation and dried at 60 °C. Collect the dried samples and name them F-BNNs-9-75nmGQDs (S1), F-BNNs-9-25nmGQDs (S2), F-BNNs-9-15nmGQDs (S3), F-BNNs-9-5nmGQDs (S4).

The structure of the samples was examined using X-ray powder diffraction (XRD, BRUKER D8 Advance) analysis. The morphology of the sample was observed using a transmission electron microscope equipped with an energy dispersive X-ray spectrometer (TEM, JEOL, JEM2010FEF, 200 kV and TEM, FEI, Talos F200S, 200 kV). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy was used to analyze the type of bonding of the samples (XPS, Thermo ESCALAB 250XI, USA). The photoluminescence spectrum spectra of all GQDs samples are recorded with a fluorescence spectrophotometer (PL, BRUKER, V80). The wettability of the sample was tested using a contact angle measuring instrument (POWEREACH, JC2000). The magnetic properties of the F-BNNSs were studied with a comprehensive physical property measurement system (PPMS, Quantum Design Inc, DynaCool, USA). The complex permittivity and permeability values were measured in the 2∼18, 19∼27 and 28∼40 GHz range with coaxial wire and waveguide method by AV3672C PNA-L Network Analyzer. The complex permittivity and complex permeability of all samples were tested by coaxial air-line and waveguide. The measured samples were prepared by uniformly mixing 20 wt% of F-BNNs and GQDs/F-BNNs with a paraffin matrix at 80 °C. The mixture was then pressed into toroidal-shaped samples with an outer inner diameter of 3.00 mm and diameter of 7.00 mm.

Our calculations were based on density functional theory (DFT) in conjunction with projector augmented wave (PAW) potentials [24], as implemented in the PWmat [25]. For the exchange-correlation functional the generalized gradient approximations (GGA) of Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) was employed for the structure relaxation and static self-consistent calculations [26]. The dispersion-corrected DFT-D3 schemes were employed to describe the possible Van der Waals (vdW) interactions [27]. The cut-off energy was set to be 500 eV. 5 × 5 × 1 supercell models with a vacuum layer larger than 20 Å in the z direction for h-BN were used to simulate F functionalization, where a Monkhorst-Pack scheme generating K-point mesh (6 × 6 × 1) was employed [28].

The crystallographic structure and phase purity of F-BNNs-1∼F-BNNs-9 (Fig. 2 a–c) were identified by X-ray diffraction. Two peaks at 2θ = 26° and 43° are attributable to the (002) and (100) planes of the graphitic structure of h-BN (PDF#34-0421), respectively, suggesting the existence of high-purity BNNs crystals [29]. Significantly, only the peak of the (002) crystal plane (Fig. 2c) shifted to the right, indicating that more F atoms were doped into BNNs to form F-BNNs. Because without causing a change in the crystal structure, the doped F atoms have a smaller lattice size than the original BNNs, causing a small shift in the peak position to the right. Therefore, it can be considered that the F-BNNs prepared under the reaction condition of 240 °C for 30 h has the greatest degree of fluorination.

The chemical bonds and elemental composition of F-BNNs-9 were further characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). In the XPS spectrum of F-BNNs-9 (Fig. 3 ), two characteristic peaks at 190.5 and 397.9 eV correspond to B 1s and N 1s, respectively (Fig. 3 b and c). As shown in Fig. 3d, three characteristic peaks at 686.9 eV correspond to F 1s [30]. The F/B ratio is about 0.006 and the B/N ratio is 1.27 from the XPS survey. O 1s peaks shown in Fig. 3a, most likely result from the exposure of F-BNNs to air or during the XPS measurement preparation. According to previous work, the F atoms attached to the surface of the BNNs, and covalently modified with some B atoms on the surface of BNNs successfully [31,32].

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was adopted to further analyze F-BNNs-9 structure. The pattern of Fig. 4 (a–c) F-BNNs-9 is not damaged during the hydrothermal process, and it has curved edges, which can determine the number of layers and thickness of these sheets. Fig. 4d shows the 0.333 nm lattice spacing and (002) crystal planes, indicating the good crystallinity of F-BNNs-9. The distribution of the four elements can be seen in Fig. 4e (B, C, N and F), indicating that F atoms are doped into the BNNs, which corresponds to the XPS test.

Fig. 5 a shows the magnetization curves of F-BNNs-1, F-BNNs-5 and F-BNNs-9 at 300 K, after subtraction of the diamagnetic background. The magnetic hysteresis loops for F-BNNs-1, F-BNNs-5 and F-BNNs-9 in a low field from −500 Oe to 500 Oe exhibit clear ferromagnetic characteristics even though the ferromagnetism is very weak. According to the basic equation of electromagnetics from (1) and (2):
(1)
M =
χ M
H
(2)
μ r
=
χ M
+ 1

M is the magnetization,
χ M
is the magnetic susceptibility, H is the magnetic field strength and μ
r is the relative magnetic permeability. According to formulas (1) and (2), the following conclusions can be drawn:
M
F − B N N s − 9
>
M
F −
B N N s − 5
>
M
F − B N N s − 1
μ r
F − B N N s − 9
>
μ r
F −
B N N s − 5
>
μ r
F − B N N s − 1

It can be seen that as the degree of fluorination of BNNs increases, its saturation magnetization will increase, and the degree of magnetization will also increase. Thus, we conclude that the observed ferromagnetism in F-BNNs-9 is induced by fluorination. tanδ
M values of F-BNNs-1∼F-BNNs-9 samples are shown in Fig. 6 o, F atom doping of F-BNNs-9 leads to the highest magnetic loss. In the process of increasing fluorination temperature and time, tanδ
M of F-BNNs continues to increase, which is consistent with the conclusion obtained in Fig. 5a. As shown in Fig. 5b, after F atoms are doped into BNNs, the electron spins of BNNs are no longer symmetrical, and the wavefunctions is highly localized at F atom and three N atoms nearest to the sp3-hybridized B atoms, accompanied by extended tails. Meanwhile, the electron energy difference between F and B atoms is larger, they prefer to combine with B atoms, which makes the sp2 hybridization of BNNs look like sp3 hybridization (Fig. 5c). Therefore, the magnetic loss of F-BNNs are significantly increased, which is beneficial to enhance the microwave absorption performance of the composite.

Comparing F-BNNs prepared at 180 °C, 210 °C and 240 °C, it is found that RL min increases slightly with the increase of the reaction time. This shows that increasing the reaction temperature and reaction time can help increase RL min of F-BNNs. (Fig. 6a–f). When the reaction temperature reaches 240 °C, it has been significantly improved, due to the influence of F atom doping on the electron spin of BNNs. As shown in Fig. 6i, the F-BNNs-9 exhibits the highest RL min value. Because, the introduction of more F atoms will cause changes in the internal electronic structure of BNNs, and the spin angular momentum of the electrons will change, it is conducive to absorbing electromagnetic wave energy to enhance microwave absorption performance.

In addition to the RL, the six physical quantities ε′, ε″, μ′, μ″, tanδ
E and tanδ
M are also important parameters for studying the absorption properties of materials [33,34]. It is noteworthy that the ε′, ε″ and tanδ
E increases with increasing fluorination in Fig. 6(j–l). This test result is consistent with the conclusion that the energy gap narrows in the energy band structure in Fig. 5b. The change in the energy band structure shows that the energy gap of F-BNNs gradually increases with the degree of fluorination. This indicates that the fluorination process is the increasing ability of electron leap, and this change is beneficial to improve the electrical loss of BNNs. Moreover, F-BNNs is a non-magnetic material, and we can get its magnetic permeability in real and imaginary parts by testing. As shown in Fig. 6(m–o), we can see that μ′, μ″ and tanδ
M of BNNs increase with the increase of fluorination, which is attributed to the imbalance of electron spins in BNNs caused by the addition of F atoms. This is consistent with the conclusions obtained in Fig. 5c. Although F-BNNs have greatly increased microwave absorption in the 14∼18 GHz frequency band, the RL in the 2∼14 GHz region is still relatively low. Therefore, how to further improve the microwave absorption performance based on maintaining the original absorption intensity and characteristics has become a key issue.

The “top-down approaches” is one of the basic methods for synthesizing GQDs. Larger graphitized carbon materials are oxidized and cracked into small-sized GQDs, commonly used materials are graphite [35], GO [36], carbon nanotubes [37] and carbon fibers [38]. For the preparation of GQDs, there are generally two major problems: i) the yield of GQDs is low, and how to improve its yield is a difficult problem; ii) although there are many synthesis methods for GQDs, it is difficult to achieve large-scale uniform and controllable synthesis.

Herein, GO is used as a precursor, GQDs of different sizes are obtained by changing the reaction time under the action of KOH. As shown in Fig. 7 a, as a precursor of GQDs, GO has the advantages of high reaction yield and mild reaction conditions. After reacted for 5 h, the morphology of GO has changed but no GQDs were formed, but the trend of GO conversion to GQDs can be seen (Fig. 7b). As shown in Fig. 7(c–f), when the reaction time continued to increase to 10 h, 15 h, 20 h and 30 h, GQDs of different sizes were prepared separately (75 nm, 25 nm, 15 nm and 5 nm). Although the size of the four GQDs is different, the morphology is the same and is evenly distributed on the surface of the carbon film, and the lattice size of GQDs (5 nm) is 0.24 nm (Fig. 7f) [15].

As the size of GQDs changes, the photoluminescence properties of GQDs have also changed. Under the excitation of light with a wavelength of 450 nm, the emission spectra of the four kinds of GQDs (75 nm, 25 nm, 15 nm and 5 nm) have blue-shifted and the emission wavelengths are 542 nm, 537 nm, 533 nm and 526 nm (Fig. 7g). As shown in Fig. 7 (h and i), the four kinds of GQDs all show light brown under visible light, while the color under ultraviolet light changes slightly, which is the same as the result of the PL spectrum test. This is attributed to the change in the bandgap width caused by the size of GQDs, the bandgap width gradually increases as its size decreases. The bandgap width increases the photon energy required for electrons to transition from the ground state energy level to the excited state energy level, and the energy absorbed from the microwave increases, which helps to improve the microwave absorption capacity [39].

To obtain a material with excellent microwave absorption properties, GQDs of different sizes are compounded with F-BNNs-9. As shown in Fig. 8 a–e, after the hydrothermal reaction, GQDs (75 nm, 25 nm, 15 nm and 5 nm) are uniformly loaded on F-BNNs-9. As shown in Fig. 8e and f, the crystal planes of GQDs and F-BNNs-9 overlap each other, the lattice fringes are very obvious, and the distribution of B, C, N and F elements is uniform.

The Z in, RL and α values of the S1, S2, S3 and S4 were calculated based on transmission line theory. The formulas are as follows [40,41]:
(3)
Z
i n
=
μ r
ε r
tanh
(
j
2 π f d
μ r
ε r
c
)
(4)
R L = 20
l g
|
Z
i n
− 1
Z
i n
+ 1
|
(5)
α =
2
π f
c
(
μ ″
ε ″
−
μ ′
ε ′
)
+
(
μ ″
ε ″
−
μ ′
ε ′
)
2
+
(
ε ′
μ ″
+
ε ″
μ ′
)
2
Where Z
in is the normalized input impedance of absorber, ε r is the relative complex permeability and permittivity, ƒ is the frequency of microwave, d is the thickness of absorber, c is the velocity of the electromagnetic wave in vacuum, RL is the energy absorbed by electromagnetic waves, α is the attenuation constant, ε′ is the real part of the relative permittivity, ε″ is the imaginary part of the relative permittivity, μ′ is the real part of the relative permeability and μ″ is the imaginary part of the relative permeability.

According to equations (3) and (4), RL is the electromagnetic wave absorption performance of samples. RL min < -10 dB indicating attenuation of more than 90% of microwave energy. It can be seen from Fig. 9 that the loading of GQDs improves the microwave absorption performance of F-BNNs-9 and the performance improvement effect of loading GQDs of different sizes is not the same. This is due to the different bandgaps of GQDs of four different sizes, which is consistent with the results of the PL spectrum test (Fig. 7g).

In addition to comparing RL min and thickness, the absorption frequency bandwidth is also an important parameter [42,43]. As shown in Fig. 10 (a–c), the absorption frequency bandwidth of S1, S2 and S3 samples are 10.2 GHz, 10.6 GHz and 11.1 GHz, respectively, and the absorption area is concentrated between about 2∼18 GHz. S4 sample has an absorption bandwidth of 4.2 GHz in 3.9∼8.1 GHz, and a strong absorption band of 7.4 GHz in 10.6∼18 GHz (Fig. 10d). The total absorption width of S4 sample in the 2∼18 GHz frequency band reached an astonishing 11.6 GHz (RL min < −10 dB), it can be seen that S4 sample has the characteristics of a strong RL min and wide absorption frequency band.

Based on the generalized transmission line theory [44–46], RL of the microwave absorption materials can be calculated using their complex relative permittivity. The RL values of different samples in 2∼18 GHz with various thicknesses from 0 to 10.0 mm are shown in Fig. 10(e–h). The RL min of S1 is −54.63 dB at 10.88 GHz with a sample thickness of 2.57 mm (Fig. 10e). For S2, due to its enhanced tanδ
E and tanδ
M (Fig. 11 e and f), the RL min of −56.88 dB at 10.64 GHz is obtained at a smaller sample thickness of 2.28 mm (Fig. 10f). For S3, the tanδ
E and tanδ
M continue to increase due to the decreasing size of GQDs. The absorption property becomes strong and RL min is −60.15 dB at 6.08 GHz with a sample thickness of 4.92 mm (Fig. 10g). When GQDs size reaches the minimum value (5 nm), F-BNNs are uniformly covered by GQDs and the loading rate reaches the maximum value. RL min of S4 is −66.69 dB at 5.6 GHz with a sample thickness of 5.29 mm (Fig. 10h), which is much higher than that of both S1, S2 and S3. Therefore, GQDs loaded on F-BNNs can effectively enhance microwave absorption performance.

ε′, ε″, μ′, μ″, tanδ
E and tanδ
M of different samples (F-BNNs-9, S1, S2, S3 and S4) measured in the frequency range of 2∼18 GHz are shown in Fig. 11. Microwave absorption properties of a dielectric and magnetic absorber are correlated with its complex permittivity and complex imaginary, where ε′ represent the storage ability of electric energy, ε″ represent the loss ability of electric energy, μ′ represent the storage ability of magnetic energy, μ″ represent the loss ability of magnetic energy, tanδ
E (tanδ
E
=ε″/ε′) represent electrical loss and tanδ
M (tanδ
M
=μ″/μ′) represent a magnetic loss. The average value of ε′ by F-BNNs-9 is less than 4 in the 2∼18 GHz (Fig. 11a), and the values of ε′ by S4 increase to 5.5 because the free carriers of GQDs enhance F-BNNs-9 electric polarization performance. The average value of ε″ changes from 0.5 to 1.0 (Fig. 11b), this result is the same as tanδ
E (Fig. 11e) and indicates that GQDs promote the loss of electrical energy in the microwave, the same trend is also reflected in the magnetic loss. When GQDs have loaded on F-BNNs-9, the spin angular momentum of F-BNNs-9 has changed, which improves its storage ability of magnetic energy. As shown in Fig. 11 (c, d), μ′ of F-BNNs-9 in the 2∼18 GHz is less than 1.5, but the values of S4 sample can achieve 2.5. Furthermore, μ″ of F-BNNs-9 and S4 exhibit an obvious increase from 0.5 to 1.5, it is consistent with the conclusion of tanδ
M image (Fig. 11f).

α is a key constant to estimate the comprehensive loss of competence for materials, which can be expressed as formula (5). Z in is a parameter to distinguish the degree of matching between μ r and ε r of the material. The closer the impedance matching rate is to 1, the better the microwave absorption performance of the material. As shown in Fig. 12 a, α of F-BNNs-9 sample is only 225 but S1, S2 and S3 samples are 288, 373 and 436, and S4 sample has the highest value of 478 among all samples. The increased tendency of values from F-BNNs-9 to S4 while the increase of the loss constant of S4 sample is determined by the increase of tanδ
E and tanδ
M together (Fig. 11e and f). Z in of S4 sample is the closest to 1 (Fig. 12b), which indicates that S4 sample has the best microwave absorption performance with the best μ r
(
μ r
=
μ ′
− j
μ ″
)
and ε r
(
ε r
=
ε ′
− j
ε ″
)
matching, which is consistent with the conclusion in Fig. 11.

To further explore the application of F-BNNs-9, S1, S2, S3 and S4 samples in the field of microwave absorption, tests were carried out in the 19∼27 and 28∼40 GHz frequency bands. As shown in Fig. 13 a, RL min of F-BNNs, S1 and S2 samples are higher than −10 dB, which cannot meet the basic requirements of microwave absorbing materials. But RL min of S3 and S4 samples are −12.5, −22.3 dB and −13.3, −22.5 dB in the 19∼26 and 27∼40 GHz, respectively (Fig. 13b). In comparison with other typical microwave absorbing materials in recent references, the microwave absorption performance including RL min and effective bandwidth of various GQDs/F-BNNs composites are better in Table 2 . A comprehensive comparison of the morphology, RL min, α and Z in of the four samples shows that S4 sample has the best microwave absorption performance, which has played a guiding role for the subsequent development of BNNs composites materials in the field of microwave absorption.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The possible mechanism of GQDs/F-BNNs for the microwave absorption
ce:section.ce:para: Here, we use F-BNNs as the main material, which neither produces skin effect nor prevents microwave from passing [55,56]. After fluorination, the electron spin changes to obtain a certain magnetic loss performance (Fig. 14 a). F-BNNs can protect GQDs to overcome the hydrophilicity, the contact angle of GQDs/F-BNNs is 77.24° (Fig. 14b). Under the action of an external microwave, the valence electron in each GQDs can be excited by the photon energy and transition from the ground state energy level to the excited state energy level (Fig. 14c). Due to the degeneracy of electronic energy levels in carbon atoms, as the size of GQDs continues to decrease, the degeneracy of their own energy levels continues to increase [57]. The increase in degeneracy enhances the transition ability of electrons after absorbing photons, thereby improving the microwave absorption performance of the material. The GQDs/F-BNNs composite material not only absorbs microwave energy but also reduces reflection and obtains excellent microwave absorption performance (Fig. 14d). When electromagnetic waves act on GQDs, the electrons in the GQDs absorb photon energy, resulting in the jump of electrons from the ground state energy level to the excited state energy level [58]. The absorption process of free carriers is a leap between electronic states within the same band. Because the free carrier absorption of photons must conform to the energy law and cannot occur indefinitely, it is accompanied by phonon scattering and impurity ionization (Fig. 14e) [59,60]. It was concluded from the PL spectroscopy test that adjusting the size of GQDs is to adjust its absorption bandwidth. Combined with the conclusions obtained in the PL spectrum test, adjusting the size of GQDs can adjust the microwave absorbing properties of the composite material.

In this work, we synthesized the GQDs/F-BNNs composites structure by one-step hydrothermal method, which has the advantages of high RL, wide absorption frequency band and strong stability, Experiments and theoretical calculations proved that the reduced microwave reflection and enhanced magnetic loss are attributed to the low dielectric constant and ferromagnetism of F-BNNs, which also overcome the typically hydrophilic of GQDs. The absorption bandwidths and RL min are closely correlated with the size of GQDs, and GQDs of 5 nm are most suitable for preparing composites materials. RL min of S4 sample is −66.69 dB in the 2∼18 GHz frequency band and an astonishing 11.6 GHz absorption frequency band. The RL in the 19∼26 and 27∼40 GHz frequency bands is also less than −10 dB, which has excellent microwave absorption performance. The success of the GQDs/F-BNNs composite has played a guiding role in the subsequent preparation and application of novel microwave absorption materials.

Qi Hu: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Yi Fang: Supervision, Project administration, Resources. Zhonglu Guo: Formal analysis, Visualization. Zhao Du: Formal analysis. Zhenya Liu: Formal analysis. Yadi Yu: Formal analysis. Xiaoyu Tian: Formal analysis. Chengchun Tang: Supervision, Project administration, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (51772075), Hebei Provincial Natural Science Foundation (E2018202129) and S&T Program of Hebei (199676242H).
